utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is more effective than wo_skills. Both produced proposals that score 1 on all six judge evaluation items at a surface level, but the quality difference is significant in process and depth. The wi_skills run: (1) invoked the doc-coauthoring skill with structured workflow; (2) performed a reader test using a subagent, which identified an ambiguity and triggered a document revision; (3) produced a longer, more detailed proposal (13,885 bytes vs 10,935 bytes); (4) explicitly surfaced and addressed the VP of Sales compensation model concern as a distinct named risk; and (5) defined escalation triggers more precisely. While both proposals would likely satisfy a casual reader, the wi_skills proposal is more likely to survive scrutiny from a skeptical VP of Sales because it went through an explicit reader perspective check and revision cycle.

utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced structurally complete, evidence-grounded proposals that address all six evaluation criteria. However, wi_skills is meaningfully higher quality for the following reasons: (1) wi_skills passed all 7 verifier checks including V4 (structured clarifying questions with 5+ numbered items before drafting) and V6 (reader test performed), while wo_skills failed both V4 and V6 — indicating the skill-guided workflow surfaced the VP of Sales political concern through structured context gathering rather than assumption, and validated the finished document against a skeptical zero-context reader before delivery; (2) the skill's reader test (V6) explicitly triggered a document revision, demonstrating that the final wi_skills proposal was actively tested and refined for persuasiveness; (3) the wo_skills agent went directly from reading source files to drafting without any brainstorming or clarifying question phase, meaning audience-specific concerns were addressed only because they happened to be in the research notes, not because of a systematic stakeholder analysis. In summary, wi_skills is more effective: it follows the structured co-authoring workflow that produces a more deliberate, audience-tested proposal, while wo_skills produces a competent but less rigorously validated document.
